name: Evidence Passing — Dictionary and Witness Compilation
summary: Wadler-Blott 1989: implicit type-class facts become first-class IR values that ordinary optimisations can specialise. A function with a `Show` constraint becomes a function with an explicit `Show` dictionary parameter. Rust traits, Haskell type classes, Swift protocols, Koka effects all use variants.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Wadler and Blott's "How to Make Ad-hoc Polymorphism Less Ad Hoc" (POPL 1989) introduced **evidence passing** (also called dictionary passing or witness passing): implicit facts about types — type-class instances, trait implementations, protocol witnesses, effect handlers — become first-class IR values that ordinary optimisations can specialise.

A function with a `Show` constraint becomes a function with an explicit `Show` dictionary parameter: a record of the methods that constraint provides. Calls site-specifically pass the dictionary; intra-procedural specialisation can inline it; the optimiser can constant-fold the methods if the dictionary is statically known.

sections.title: Production uses
sections.content:
GHC compiles Haskell type classes via dictionary passing. Rust traits use a similar dictionary-passing approach for `dyn Trait` (with monomorphisation as the alternative for `impl Trait`). Swift protocols use witness tables — a dictionary-passing variant where the witness table is a thin pointer-vector rather than a structural record.

Koka's effect handlers use evidence passing to thread handler references through every effectful operation, avoiding continuation capture for the common case of tail-resumptive handlers (Xie & Leijen, ICFP 2021).

The lesson: **once implicit semantic facts (instances, witnesses, handlers) become explicit IR values, the rest of the optimiser is almost free**. Inlining, constant propagation, dead-code elimination all act on the dictionary just as they would on any other IR value. The hard work is the elaboration that produced the dictionary in the first place.

sources.label: Generalized Evidence Passing for Effect Handlers (Xie & Leijen, ICFP 2021)
